items.title: ERP hits -70bps — first negative equity risk premium since June 2002
items.idea: S&P 500 earnings yield at ~3.7% vs 10-year at 4.4% = ERP of -70bps. In 40 years of monthly data, there is not a single instance where the ERP went negative and forward 24-month real equity returns were positive. Mean forward real return from negative-ERP starts: -14.6% with std dev of 11.2%. The 2002 analog (ERP touched zero) triggered -23.4% drawdown in 4 months. The 2007 analog triggered -57% peak-to-trough with gold running +191%.
items.angle: This is the single most important chart right now. The ERP going negative doesn't mean sell tomorrow — but the historical base rate is brutal and the setup rhymes with the two worst bear markets since WWII.
items.tweetA: The S&P earnings yield is 3.7%. The 10-year is 4.4%. You are now being paid a PENALTY to own stocks over Treasuries. ERP: -70bps. In 40 years of data, not one single instance where this ended with positive real equity returns over 24 months. Not one.
items.tweetB: Last time the equity risk premium went negative: June 2002. What followed: S&P -23% in 4 months, 10yr rallied 183bps, gold +14%, EUR/USD +8.6%. Before that: 2007. S&P -57%, gold +191%. The current setup has both analogs on the table simultaneously.

charts.desc: ERP chart showing the green arrow marking the first sub-zero reading since June 2002 — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: 30-year Treasury auction stops at 5.046% — first 5-handle since June 2007
items.idea: Wednesday's 30-year bond auction cleared at 5.046%, the first time a long-bond auction has printed a 5-handle since June 2007. The 10-year closed the week at 4.597%, up 13.8bps Friday alone. The 30-year hit 5.122%. The author describes the moment as understanding 'what happens when the long end of the curve stops being a safe allocation and becomes a price-takers' market.'
items.angle: The long end is becoming a price-takers market. This is not about the level — 4.6% is not historically exceptional — it's about the dynamic: no buyers at these prices, with the global backdrop (JGB highs, UK gilts) making it worse.
items.tweetA: The 30-year Treasury auction cleared at 5.046% Wednesday. First 5-handle on a long-bond auction since June 2007. The 30-year yield hit 5.122% Friday. JGBs at 29-year highs. German bunds at 20-year highs. This is a global bond problem, not a US one.
items.tweetB: A 5% 30-year yield sounds manageable until you price what it does to equity duration. At ERP of -70bps, every basis point that moves higher in the long bond makes the risk-free alternative more attractive. The long end is beginning to discipline everything.

items.title: May PPI +6.0% YoY — hottest in 3 years, as Warsh inherits the problem
items.idea: May PPI came in at +6.0% year-over-year, the hottest print in three years, arriving the same week Kevin Warsh was sworn in as Fed Chair. The author argues the print gives Warsh 'political cover to validate the hawkish market path at the June meeting.' The market is currently pricing roughly 35% of the realistic hawkish-Warsh trajectory, leaving significant upside in the front end of the curve.
items.angle: Warsh gets a hot PPI on Day 1. The market is only pricing 35% of where he might go. Front-end rates are not done moving.
items.tweetA: PPI +6.0% YoY. Hottest in 3 years. Warsh was sworn in Friday. The market is pricing 35% of the realistic hawkish path. Either inflation breaks fast or the front end has a lot more repricing to do. The June meeting just got very interesting.
items.tweetB: Warsh inherits: PPI +6.0%, 30yr at 5.05%, ERP negative, OIS curve pricing zero cuts. The Fed's March dot plot showed 2 cuts by September 2027. The market says one hike. The gap is 73bps — widest since November 2007. Someone is very wrong.

items.title: Market-Fed gap: 73bps — widest since November 2007
items.idea: The OIS curve prices the funds rate at 3.855% by June 2027 (zero cuts, roughly one hike) vs the Fed's March dot plot of 3.125% (2 cuts). The gap at the 18-month forward node is 73bps — the widest market-Fed disagreement since November 2007. The rolling correlation between this gap and the 18-month forward funds rate is +0.81. In Nov 2007 the Fed was projecting 4.50%, market priced 3.10%; funds rate hit 0.25% by Dec 2008.
items.angle: The market is pricing something the Fed has not accepted yet. The historical track record says the market tends to be right on direction even when wrong on magnitude. This is a front-end rates trade.
items.tweetA: Fed projects 2 cuts by Sept 2027. Market prices zero cuts and one hike. The gap: 73bps at the 18-month forward node. The last time this gap was this wide: November 2007. What happened next you know. The question is which direction it resolves this time.
items.tweetB: In Sept 2021 the Fed was projecting 0.25% end-2022. Market was pricing 1.20%. The funds rate hit 4.50%. In Nov 2007 the Fed projected 4.50%. The funds rate hit 0.25%. The market is directionally right but often wrong on magnitude. Current gap: 73bps. Act accordingly.

charts.desc: OIS vs Fed dot plot divergence chart — widest gap since Nov 2007 — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Hormuz: WTI $101, Brent $108, up 45% since Feb 28 war — 1,600 vessels trapped
items.idea: Oil resumed its climb Friday with WTI near $101 and Brent near $108, both up more than 45% since the war began on February 28. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned the global oil market would not normalize until 2027 if the Strait of Hormuz remained blocked beyond mid-June. 1,600 vessels are currently trapped. US diesel above $5/gallon, gasoline above $4. One-third of global seaborne fertilizer trade disrupted during critical spring planting season.
items.angle: The fertilizer disruption during spring planting is the most underappreciated second-order effect. Food inflation in 6-12 months is baked in unless Hormuz opens before June.
items.tweetA: 1,600 vessels trapped. Oil up 45% since February 28. Diesel above $5. But the Aramco CEO's warning is the one to watch: 'market won't normalize until 2027 if Hormuz stays blocked past mid-June.' One-third of global seaborne fertilizer is currently disrupted. During spring planting.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching oil. Nobody's pricing the fertilizer disruption. One-third of global seaborne fertilizer trade is blocked at Hormuz. Spring planting window is open. Food inflation has a 6-12 month lag. The commodity supercycle story just got a second leg.

items.title: Empire State Mfg Index: 19.6 vs 7.0 consensus — Fed hike odds jump to 45%
items.idea: Empire State Manufacturing Index leaped to 19.6 in May, the highest reading since April 2022 and far above the 7.0 consensus estimate. CME FedWatch showed the probability of a Fed rate hike in 2026 climbing to 45%, up from just 1% one month ago, as war-related inflation increasingly worried investors. The 10-year yield spiked 9bps Friday to 4.55%, its highest in a year.
items.angle: One month ago, hike odds were 1%. Now they're 45%. This is not a gradual repricing — this is a regime change in expectations happening in real time. The front end is not done moving.
items.tweetA: Fed hike odds: 1% one month ago. 45% today. Empire State Mfg printed 19.6 vs 7.0 consensus — highest since April 2022. The bond market is not pricing a soft landing anymore. It's pricing a policy error in the other direction.
items.tweetB: The market went from pricing 6 cuts at the start of 2026 to pricing a hike. That's a 700bps+ swing in expectations in months. If you're still positioned for the rate-cut cycle, you're fighting the most aggressive macro repricing since 2022.

items.title: Karsan: 17% rally in 6 weeks is one of 9 rarest moves in a century — markets ARE the policy
items.idea: Cem Karsan of Kai Volatility Advisors: only 9 rallies of that size and speed have occurred in 100 years, and all 8 others followed declines of at least 20% — this pullback was only 9%. Public equity is $150T, tied assets $300T. A 20% rally generates ~$60T in new collateral, 'an order of magnitude larger than the liquidity injected during the pandemic.' 50% of US consumption comes from the top 10% of the wealth distribution, so equity wealth IS economic activity.
items.angle: Karsan's framing reframes why the administration talked markets up so aggressively post-Liberation Day — it wasn't PR, it was monetary policy by another means.
items.tweetA: Why did the administration work so hard to talk markets back up? Karsan explains: public equity is $150T. A 20% rally = $60T in new collateral. That's an order of magnitude bigger than everything the Fed injected during COVID. Markets aren't just a scoreboard — they ARE the liquidity mechanism.
items.tweetB: Only 9 rallies of 17%+ in 6 weeks in the last 100 years of market history. All 8 prior instances followed drops of at least 20%. This one followed a 9% pullback. The market is pricing a historic recovery from a correction. Karsan thinks the late-2026 window carries the real downside risk.

items.title: Gary Shilling: 20-30% crash call — top 10% consumer spending is the trigger
items.idea: Gary Shilling (called the 1969 recession, 1970s inflation unwind, 2006 housing bubble) says the economy leaned too heavily on high-income consumers. Any pullback in that cohort — especially after a sharp equity selloff — cascades through the broader economy. He predicts a 2026 recession and 20-30% stock correction. Also structurally bearish on China (property collapse, thin domestic demand) and prefers India on a long-horizon basis.
items.angle: The wealth effect working in reverse is the mechanism. If the ERP going negative triggers a correction, the top 10% who drove 50% of consumption pull back simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Gary Shilling, who called the 1969 recession and the 2006 housing bubble, is positioned firmly risk-off. His mechanism: 50% of US consumption comes from the top 10%. An equity selloff + wealth effect reversal = recession trigger. Long Treasuries, short equities.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this: if the top 10% drive 50% of US consumption, and they drive it through wealth effects, and the ERP just went negative for the first time since 2002 — then the recession model isn't about employment, it's about portfolios. Shilling sees 20-30% before this resolves.

items.title: Bonds 'have the smell of a crash' — TLT targets $82, $73, and $52 (financial crisis scenario)
items.idea: Dale Pinkert: 10-year at 4.597%, 30-year at 5.122%. TLT was $93 in March. Near-term target $82 ('we could be there Monday'). Base case formation from March measures to $73. Third drive line at $52 — 'that's a financial crisis. I don't know what conditions besides outright deflation is going to give me a bull case for bonds at this point.' He describes TLT as 'trading like a penny stock' — bonds used to be the most liquid market.
items.angle: TLT at $52 would be a 44% decline from the March high. That's the kind of loss that breaks pension funds, insurance company balance sheets, and any portfolio that treated bonds as the safe sleeve.
items.tweetA: TLT was $93 in March. Pinkert's near-term target: $82. Base case: $73. Third drive: $52 — 'that's a financial crisis.' He says bonds are now 'trading like a penny stock.' The most liquid market in the world is becoming a price-takers' market. Watch $82 Monday.
items.tweetB: The bond market has a $52 scenario on the table. Not base case — but it's on the chart. JGBs at 29-year highs. UK gilts struggling. 30-year at 5.12%. Pinkert: 'It's a global bond problem.' The interesting question: what breaks first — bonds, or equity multiples?

charts.desc: TLT chart with multiple downside targets ($82, $73, $52 financial crisis level) — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: S&P path: correction to 7,100-6,800 (Wave 2), then melt-up to ~8,000 (Wave 3)
items.idea: Pinkert's Elliott Wave read on the S&P: we are in a pause after Wave 1 of a new leg. Wave 2 correction to 7,100 (worst case 6,800), then Wave 3 runs to near 8,000 — the 'monster.' Crucially, he says the bull market ends this year. His historical parallel: 1987, when rates kept rising and markets kept rising simultaneously until the rubber band snapped. The disintermediation scenario — equities and bonds both selling off — is underpriced.
items.angle: The 1987 parallel is key: if you can have rates rising and stocks rising simultaneously (we had this in 2021-2022 briefly), the question is not will it end but when. Pinkert says this year.
items.tweetA: Pinkert's S&P framework: Wave 2 dip to 7,100-6,800, then a Wave 3 melt-up toward 8,000. Then the bull market ends this year. His parallel: 1987 — rates were rising, stocks were rising, and nobody knew where the rubber band would snap until it did. Watch the bond-equity correlation.
items.tweetB: A path to S&P 8,000 and a path to 'bull market ends 2026' are not contradictory. They're sequential. The question is whether you're positioned for the melt-up, the peak recognition, or the aftermath. Right now the market is somewhere in Wave 2.

charts.desc: Elliott Wave chart with Wave 2 correction zones and Wave 3 target near 8,000

items.title: Ironsides: energy spike = margin compression, not consumer inflation — Warsh privatizes the balance sheet
items.idea: Ironsides argues this week's CPI/PPI/import price spikes do not signal lasting inflation. Instead, higher energy prices are squeezing profit margins (like tariffs did in 2025) and weakening demand — which is disinflationary, not inflationary. The energy price spike impacts margins rather than getting passed through to consumer prices. Warsh's mandate includes 'privatization of the balance sheet' — reducing the Fed's footprint — but the energy-driven deficit delay makes the timeline harder. Ironsides is reducing risk in economically sensitive equities and increasing short-term Treasuries/cash.
items.angle: This is the contrarian macro read: the inflation headlines are actually a bearish signal for economically sensitive cyclicals, not a hawkish signal for the Fed. Margin compression ≠ CPI persistence.
items.tweetA: Ironsides' contrarian read on inflation week: the CPI and PPI prints are NOT persistent inflation — they're energy squeezing margins, which is ultimately disinflationary as demand weakens. Same playbook as tariffs in 2025. The victim is cyclical earnings, not the Fed's credibility.
items.tweetB: If energy prices eat margins rather than get passed through, the PPI number is a sell signal for economically sensitive sectors, not a buy signal for commodities. Ironsides is cutting cyclical equity exposure and adding short-dated Treasuries. The recession trade is being set up quietly.

items.title: Both McClellan Summation Indexes rolled over; Citi Panic/Euphoria at 'Euphoric'
items.idea: Helene's Saturday chart fest: both the S&P and Nasdaq McClellan Summation Indexes have now rolled over, which is a breadth deterioration signal beneath the surface-level index strength. Separately, Citi's Panic/Euphoria model is reading 'Euphoric' — historically a warning level for near-term returns. Bank Index has been terrible but didn't make a lower low on Friday. Homebuilder ETF (ITB) on new low list. Semiconductors look 'heavy' across the board.
items.angle: The breadth story beneath ATH prices is deteriorating. When the Summation Indexes roll over while indexes are near highs, that's not a comforting signal.
items.tweetA: Beneath the S&P ATH: both McClellan Summation Indexes (S&P and Nasdaq) have rolled over. Citi's Panic/Euphoria model is at 'Euphoric.' Homebuilders on new low list. Semis 'heavy.' The index is strong. The internals are not. That divergence doesn't usually resolve bullishly.
items.tweetB: Citi Panic/Euphoria at Euphoric. McClellan Summations rolling over. Banks refusing to make new highs. This is what distribution looks like during a melt-up. You can have both — index going higher AND breadth deteriorating — until you can't.

charts.desc: Multiple sector and breadth charts including Summation Index rollover and Citi Panic/Euphoria — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Goldman: token consumption to reach 24x current global capacity by 2030; Token Economics Turn Positive 1H26
items.idea: Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research models that consumer and enterprise agents will push monthly token consumption to ~24x current global capacity by 2030. The crucial inflection: 'Token Economics Turn Positive in 1H26' — the moment any operator can deploy an agent whose output value exceeds its compute cost. When that happens, every company in the world will need to implement AI agents to compete. This is the inflection that makes the AI capex cycle not optional.
items.angle: 1H26 token economics turning positive is the most important near-term AI infrastructure catalyst. Once deployment ROI is positive, enterprise adoption goes from optional to competitive necessity.
items.tweetA: Goldman's model: token consumption reaches 24x current global capacity by 2030. But the line that matters is 'Token Economics Turn Positive 1H26.' That's the moment deploying an agent becomes cheaper than not deploying one. After that threshold, AI adoption is not a choice — it's competitive survival.
items.tweetB: 24x token consumption by 2030. But we're still in the hobbyist phase. Goldman marks 1H26 as the inflection: when agent output value exceeds compute cost. Every enterprise that hasn't deployed by then faces a productivity gap vs competitors who did. The GPU demand story doesn't end at training.

charts.desc: Goldman token consumption trajectory with 'Token Economics Turn Positive 1H26' marker — 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Agentic AI: 800K-2M tokens per task vs 50-200K for coding; CPU:GPU ratio shifted 1:12 → 1:2
items.idea: A developer coding session (Cursor) uses 50K-200K tokens. A financial analyst asking Claude to reconcile a file, draft an email summary, and prep slides uses 800K-2M tokens — 10x more. Multiply by 1.4 billion office workers. The infrastructure implication: server CPU-to-GPU ratio has already shifted from 1:12 in old training setups to 1:2 in agentic inference builds. Anthropic is now generally available inside Excel, PowerPoint, and Word (Outlook in public beta) as of May 7.
items.angle: The shift from 1:12 to 1:2 CPU:GPU ratio is the single most concrete infrastructure implication of the agentic transition. This is a massive CPU demand signal that the market hasn't fully priced.
items.tweetA: A coding assistant uses 50-200K tokens. An agentic financial workflow uses 800K-2M. Now multiply by 1.4 billion knowledge workers. The infrastructure implication: CPU-to-GPU ratio in agentic inference builds is already 1:2, down from 1:12 in training setups. The CPU supercycle is being set up right now.
items.tweetB: Copilot has 20M paying users. 80%+ of Fortune 500 has rolled it out to part of their workforce. Claude just went generally available inside Excel, Word, and PowerPoint. The TAM isn't 25M developers anymore — it's 1.4 billion knowledge workers. Every one of those sessions uses 10x more compute.

charts.desc: Agentic vs chatbot token consumption comparison chart

items.title: 78% of humanity has never used any AI tool — we are in the first inning
items.idea: Despite feeling like AI is ubiquitous, approximately 78% of humanity has never used any AI tool. The perception of saturation is driven entirely by the tech-Twitter bubble. The actual TAM for AI adoption is effectively untapped across most of the world. Figure AI just ran a 24-hour live stream featuring an autonomous humanoid robot picking and sorting packages — physical agents are just beginning.
items.angle: The bubble narrative is premature when 78% of humans haven't touched the product. The analog: in 2005, 80% of humans had never used a search engine. Nobody called that a bubble top.
items.tweetA: 78% of humanity has never used any AI tool. The next time someone tells you AI is overhyped, remind them: in the last tech cycle that felt 'overhyped,' 80% of humanity had never used a search engine either. We are in the first inning, not the ninth.
items.tweetB: Feels like AI is everywhere. 78% of humans have never touched it. Coding assistants serve 25M developers. The real opportunity is 1.4 billion knowledge workers — and beyond them, physical agents handling the other 2 billion jobs. Figure AI just did a 24-hour humanoid robot livestream. This is not late-cycle.

items.title: DeepSeek V4 at 1M context: 27% of inference FLOPs and 10% of KV cache vs V3.2
items.idea: DeepSeek V4-Pro achieves 27% of the inference FLOPs and 10% of the KV cache size of DeepSeek V3.2 at 1M-token context length, through two novel mechanisms: Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC) that widen the residual stream with stability constraints, and CSA/HCA attention that compresses along the sequence dimension rather than per-token. DeepSeek V4-Flash is even more aggressive at 10% of FLOPs and 7% of KV cache.
items.angle: Each generation of open-weight models is doing more with less. The cost curve for long-context inference is collapsing faster than anyone projected. This has direct implications for when token economics turn positive.
items.tweetA: DeepSeek V4 at 1M-token context: 27% of inference FLOPs, 10% of KV cache compared to V3.2. Flash variant: 10% FLOPs, 7% KV cache. They're compressing the sequence dimension itself, not just per-token representation. The cost of long-context inference is collapsing quarter over quarter.
items.tweetB: The most important trend nobody is tracking: open-weight inference efficiency improving ~4x every 6 months. DeepSeek V4 just ran at 27% of V3.2's FLOPs at 1M context. When inference is this cheap, Goldman's '1H26 token economics positive' thesis arrives ahead of schedule.

charts.desc: DeepSeek V4 architecture diagram with CSA/HCA and mHC components

items.title: ZAYA1-8B trained on AMD GPUs — first notable production model not on NVIDIA
items.idea: Zyphra's ZAYA1-8B was trained on AMD GPUs rather than the standard NVIDIA GPU or Google TPU setup. It also introduces Compressed Convolutional Attention (CCA), which unlike MLA-style compression that reduces per-token KV, performs attention directly in a compressed latent space — reducing both KV cache AND inference FLOPs simultaneously. CCA reportedly outperforms MLA under comparable compression settings per the standalone CCA paper.
items.angle: AMD training a notable production model is a data point in the GPU monopoly debate. If CCA outperforms MLA and is trainable on AMD, that's two signals at once.
items.tweetA: ZAYA1-8B was trained on AMD GPUs. Not NVIDIA. Not Google TPU. This is notable for one simple reason: if AMD can train competitive production LLMs, the NVIDIA moat is about training dominance, not an absolute monopoly. One data point — but watch for more.
items.tweetB: ZAYA1-8B introduces CCA: attention computed directly in a compressed latent space, reducing BOTH KV cache and inference FLOPs. The standalone CCA paper claims it outperforms MLA at comparable compression. If this holds, it's a new attention paradigm — not just an efficiency tweak.

charts.desc: CCA architecture diagram showing compressed latent attention computation

items.title: Claude Code fully self-maintained — humans are now the orchestration layer
items.idea: Anthropic says Claude Code has crossed from 'mostly self-written' to 'fully self-maintained,' with humans supervising systems instead of writing features directly. The operational shift matters more than the milestone: software teams are starting to look like orchestration layers for autonomous agents. This is the first time an AI company has publicly claimed a flagship developer tool is now fully autonomous in its own maintenance.
items.angle: This is the canary in the coal mine for software engineering labor demand. When the tool that writes code also maintains itself, the number of humans needed per line of shipped code starts collapsing.
items.tweetA: Anthropic: Claude Code is now fully self-maintained. Humans supervise systems. The milestone isn't the headline — the operational model is. Software teams are becoming orchestration layers. The question is not whether this applies to your company. It's when.
items.tweetB: The first fully self-maintaining commercial software tool just went live. Claude Code. What does this do to the demand signal for junior software engineers? Not immediately — but the trend line just bent. The software labor market hasn't priced this yet.

items.title: Palo Alto: 3-5 month window before AI-assisted attacks become standard procedure
items.idea: Palo Alto Networks warns companies may have only 3-5 months before AI-assisted attacks become standard operating procedure. Internal testing showed frontier models outperforming expectations in discovering unknown software vulnerabilities and exploit paths. Separately, BaFin (German regulator) is creating rapid-response inspection teams for IT audits inside financial institutions specifically because 'AI systems can scan and exploit banking weaknesses faster than existing security processes can respond.'
items.angle: The security industry is telling you the window to harden systems is Q3 2026. This is both a threat and a business opportunity — cybersecurity spending is about to accelerate sharply.
items.tweetA: Palo Alto: 3-5 months before AI-assisted attacks become standard. Not years — months. Their internal testing shows frontier models are better at finding zero-days than expected. BaFin is creating rapid-response inspection teams for banks right now. The attack surface for every organization just changed permanently.
items.tweetB: Google stopped hackers using AI to identify zero-days and bypass 2FA at scale — China and North Korea. Palo Alto says we have 3-5 months before this becomes table stakes for every attacker. The defense side of cybersecurity just got a very clear catalyst. $PANW $CRWD $S

items.title: Anthropic passes OpenAI in US business spend — corporate card data
items.idea: Corporate card data now shows Anthropic ahead of OpenAI in US enterprise adoption share after a year of aggressive expansion into developer and enterprise workflows. The numbers likely exclude major cloud-bundled contracts, which means headline market share still understates how fragmented enterprise usage is. Separately, Anthropic is hiring a 'Claude Evangelist' — signaling distribution through technical community influence rather than traditional sales.
items.angle: If Anthropic is leading in enterprise credit card spend, that's organic preference — not bundled or subsidized. This is the metric that matters more than ChatGPT MAU.
items.tweetA: Corporate card data: Anthropic now leads OpenAI in US business spend. Not a poll. Not MAU. Actual dollar flows from enterprise credit cards. After $100B+ from Microsoft and a 2-year head start, OpenAI is being outspent organically. The enterprise race is not over.
items.tweetB: Anthropic leads in US enterprise credit card spend. Microsoft has $100B+ in OpenAI. Satya Nadella's internal emails worried about becoming a commodity provider. Goldman says semiconductor profits have peaked. The AI value chain is shifting from infrastructure to inference and applications. $MSFT faces this directly.

items.title: IREN (Bitcoin miner) closes $3B convertible note to pivot into AI cloud infra
items.idea: IREN, a Bitcoin mining company, closed a $3 billion convertible note offering to push into AI cloud infrastructure. This is one of the largest single-company pivots from Bitcoin mining to AI compute seen in the sector. The move reflects both the continued attractiveness of AI infrastructure capital markets and the pressure on pure-play Bitcoin miners as the halving compresses mining economics.
items.angle: Bitcoin miners pivoting to AI cloud infra is becoming a structural trend. IREN at $3B is the largest such move yet. The energy + power infrastructure is the shared asset — the use case just changed.
items.tweetA: IREN just closed a $3B convertible note offering to pivot into AI cloud infrastructure. $3 billion. From Bitcoin mining. This is the biggest bet yet on the thesis that energy infrastructure built for mining is more valuable redeployed for AI inference. The halving is accelerating this transition.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin miners have cheap power, lots of it, in buildings optimized for heat dissipation. AI training needs the same thing. Post-halving, the economics of mining compressed. The economics of AI cloud stayed strong. $IREN just put $3B behind this arbitrage. Watch who follows.

items.source: Not Boring / Packy McCormick + Founder's Corner
items.title: Cowboy Space Corp: Aetherflux rebrands, raises $275M for orbital AI data centers with 800 GPUs
items.idea: Robinhood co-founder Baiju Bhatt's company Aetherflux rebranded as Cowboy Space Corporation and raised $275M to build rockets whose upper stages unfold into orbital AI data centers. Each satellite carries 800 GPUs with integrated power systems. The company was previously focused on energy in space before pivoting to orbital compute. Packy McCormick notes the branding is deliberately weird — cowboys in space, tumbleweed footage — as a product of 'the Great Differentiation' where attention is the scarce resource.
items.angle: Orbital data centers with 800 GPUs per satellite. The physics are compelling (no cooling costs, 24/7 solar, no real estate). The question is whether orbital latency makes them usable for inference or only for training batch jobs.
items.tweetA: Robinhood money → Cowboy Space Corporation. Baiju Bhatt raised $275M to build rockets whose upper stages unfold into orbital data centers. 800 GPUs per satellite. In space. No cooling costs. 24/7 solar. The AI infrastructure arms race just went orbital.
items.tweetB: Cowboy Space Corp is either the peak of the AI infrastructure bubble or the opening move of the next chapter. The case for orbital compute: no cooling, unlimited solar, no zoning, no real estate. The case against: latency. For batch training jobs, the physics might actually work.

items.title: Microsoft court filings: Nadella feared OpenAI reduces MSFT to 'commodity infrastructure provider'
items.idea: Court filings revealed Satya Nadella worried OpenAI could eventually reduce Microsoft to a commodity infrastructure provider — despite investing over $100 billion. Microsoft now lacks exclusivity and still has no defining AI product breakout. Separately, Goldman Sachs believes semiconductor profits have peaked while hyperscalers still carry rising infrastructure costs. The next phase depends on enterprise customers generating measurable returns from AI deployments.
items.angle: Nadella's worst case is becoming the normalized expectation. Being the cloud provider for a competitor's AGI isn't a moat — it's a tollroad that can be rerouted.
items.tweetA: Satya Nadella's private fear: OpenAI makes Microsoft a commodity infrastructure provider. $100B+ invested. No exclusivity. No defining AI product. The court filings make it public. The question for $MSFT shareholders: what is this $100B actually buying if the model goes elsewhere?
items.tweetB: Goldman: semiconductor profits have peaked, hyperscalers face rising costs, value shifts to enterprise ROI. Nadella fears becoming a commodity cloud for OpenAI. Both things can be true simultaneously — and together they describe exactly why $MSFT's AI bet is structurally precarious.

items.title: SoftBank: nearly all $46B annual gain came from one position — OpenAI stake
items.idea: Nearly all of SoftBank Vision Fund's $46 billion annual gain came from the appreciation of its OpenAI stake. Ratings agencies are warning that concentrated exposure and debt-heavy financing increase downside risk if sentiment shifts. This is a $46B bet on a single privately-held company that is now in a transition to for-profit status.
items.angle: SoftBank's $46B 'annual gain' is mark-to-model on a private stake. If OpenAI's valuation gets pressured — by regulatory action, competitive dynamics, or the IPO process — the Vision Fund balance sheet looks very different.
items.tweetA: SoftBank Vision Fund: $46B annual gain. Nearly all of it: one position, OpenAI, marked up on paper. Ratings agencies are flagging concentration risk. This is the Vision Fund 1 WeWork problem in reverse — but the mark-to-model risk goes both ways.
items.tweetB: SoftBank's entire $46B 'gain' is one private company stake at a valuation set by the last funding round. OpenAI is transitioning to for-profit. A new class of investors will set the price. If that price resets 20% lower, SoftBank's 'gain' disappears. Concentration is not a strategy.

items.title: Saylor: BTC supply is 450 coins/day (~$10-12B/yr) — any $10B credit creation absorbs full annual supply
items.idea: Strategy's STRC has a break-even rate of 2.3% — Bitcoin needs to appreciate only 2.3% annually to cover the dividend indefinitely. Bitcoin has appreciated ~40% annually. Saylor's model: BTC supply is organically constrained to 450 coins/day = ~$10-12B annually. Each marginal $10B of credit creation (banks, STRC issuance, IBIT inflows) absorbs the entire annual supply. Monthly dividend obligation $80-90M vs multi-billion dollar capital raises. Strategy issued 60% of ALL preferred stock in the US this year.
items.angle: The math is striking: every $10B of new institutional credit creation absorbs Bitcoin's entire annual supply. With STRC scaling toward Saylor's stated goal of $100B/month, the supply constraint becomes the price mechanism.
items.tweetA: The math behind Saylor's Bitcoin machine: annual BTC supply is 450 coins/day = ~$10-12B/yr. Each $10B of new credit creation (STRC, IBIT, banks) absorbs the entire annual supply. Strategy issued 60% of all US preferred stock this year. At $100B/month ambition, this becomes the Bitcoin price floor.
items.tweetB: STRC break-even: 2.3%. Bitcoin's annual appreciation: ~40%. Saylor is stripping the first 11% to fund dividends and pocketing the rest in BTC. The instrument's Sharpe ratio is reportedly 2.5-3, above NVDA and the S&P 500. This is either the greatest financial engineering in history or the most complex margin call.

items.title: Terpin: 60% probability BTC bottoms ~$42K in October; shorts current $80Ks
items.idea: Michael Terpin (Transform Ventures, Bitcoin Supercycle author): halving cycle intact, 60% probability Bitcoin makes a final low near $42,000 in October 2026. Bottoms have historically taken ~1 year to form after the initial ATH. Current downward pressure is from liquidations (not long-term holders selling). His fund is shorting the $80K range while hedging long at 2:1 odds. The downside won't extend below $40K because Strategy's buying and ETF flows have created a structural floor.
items.angle: The most interesting part: Terpin is simultaneously short AND calling for a $1M long-term target. He's not bearish on Bitcoin — he's playing the cycle. Short the $80K range, cover at $42K, reload.
items.tweetA: Terpin (cited in the Bitcoin whitepaper, $1M BTC long-term target) is SHORTING the current $80K range. His thesis: 60% probability of a final cycle low near $42K in October. The halving cycle says bottoms take ~1 year to form. The pattern is intact. He's not bearish — he's early.
items.tweetB: The Jane Street 10am dump pattern on Bitcoin? Terpin says it stopped once the pattern went public. His description of the mechanics: buy OTC, short on smaller exchanges to trigger arbitrage-driven cascades, close short and collect the spread. Bitcoin's regulatory gray zone made this possible.

items.title: ERC-7730 Clear Signing launched: structural fix for blind signing — 40+ protocols live
items.idea: The Ethereum Foundation's Trillion Dollar Security Initiative and a working group (Ledger, Trezor, Fireblocks, WalletConnect) launched Clear Signing, an open standard (ERC-7730) making human-readable transactions the default on Ethereum. Protocols write a JSON descriptor to an open registry; wallets fetch and display plain-language signing requests. Already live: Ledger, with 40+ protocols including Aave, Lido, Uniswap, and Safe. Blind signing was the attack vector for Bybit ($1.5B), WazirX ($230M), and Radiant Capital ($50M).
items.angle: This is structural infrastructure — not a feature, not a PR move. Blind signing was a systemic vulnerability that could not be solved by user education alone. ERC-7730 is the fix that makes self-custody viable at scale.
items.tweetA: Blind signing was the attack vector for $1.5B Bybit, $230M WazirX, $50M Radiant Capital. ERC-7730 just launched: an open standard that makes human-readable transaction signing the default on Ethereum. Ledger live. 40+ protocols including Aave, Lido, Uniswap. The structural vulnerability just got its structural fix.
items.tweetB: The Ethereum Foundation's Trillion Dollar Security Initiative deployed its first major output: ERC-7730 Clear Signing. This matters more than most ETH upgrades. Self-custody at scale requires users to know what they're signing. Now they will. This is what Ethereum building for its users looks like.

items.title: Institutional ETH avalanche: Bitmine holds 4.3% of supply; Fidelity tokenizes a fund; JPMorgan deploys 2nd MMF
items.idea: Multiple institutional Ethereum developments this week: Bitmine now holds 4.3% of the entire ETH supply and is slowing accumulation to below 100K ETH/week. Charles Schwab opened ETH trading to its first wave of retail investors. Fidelity is debuting FILQ, its first tokenized fund as an ERC-20 token. JPMorgan is deploying its second tokenized money market fund on Ethereum. Smart wallet upgrades at all-time highs. Uniswap added payment flow support to its API.
items.angle: Fidelity + JPMorgan tokenizing funds on Ethereum simultaneously is the institutional adoption signal that the 'ETH as settlement layer' thesis needed. These are not experiments — they are production deployments.
items.tweetA: This week in institutional Ethereum: Fidelity debuts FILQ (first tokenized fund as an ERC-20). JPMorgan deploys its second tokenized MMF on Ethereum. Charles Schwab opens ETH to retail. Bitmine holds 4.3% of all ETH. The tokenization of finance is happening on Ethereum, not in a press release.
items.tweetB: Bitmine holds 4.3% of all ETH supply and is slowing to below 100K ETH/week. Fidelity is tokenizing a fund. JPMorgan is on its second tokenized MMF on Ethereum. Meanwhile Solana monthly DEX volume vs ETH just hit a 1-year low. The 'Ethereum is dead' narrative aged poorly.

items.title: Solana monthly DEX volume ratio vs Ethereum falls to 1-year low
items.idea: Solana's monthly DEX volume ratio relative to Ethereum fell to a one-year low this week. This comes as Ethereum institutional adoption accelerates (Fidelity tokenized fund, JPMorgan 2nd MMF, Schwab ETH access). The narrative battle for DeFi dominance is shifting back toward Ethereum as institutional activity requires settlement finality and EVM composability.
items.angle: The Solana vs Ethereum DEX volume narrative peaked around the memecoin cycle. Now that memecoin activity has faded and institutional DeFi is scaling on ETH, the ratio is reverting.
items.tweetA: Solana's monthly DEX volume vs Ethereum: 1-year low. The memecoin cycle drove the narrative. Now it's fading. Meanwhile Fidelity and JPMorgan are tokenizing funds on Ethereum. Institutional DeFi runs on settlement finality and EVM. The market is now voting with volume.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this chart: Solana DEX volume share vs Ethereum at a 1-year low, the same week Fidelity and JPMorgan both launch tokenized products on Ethereum. The memecoin narrative inflected. The institutional narrative is just starting.

items.title: Nat Gas: Pinkert long — 'sleeping market' that just broke out, nobody likes it
items.idea: Dale Pinkert has been long Natural Gas for three consecutive weeks. His thesis: 'Never sell a sleeping market and it was sleeping for a quarter. So this is where I'm long.' He prefers Nat Gas over oil because it offers a lower-risk entry after the long consolidation and breakout. He describes it as 'the better, lower-risk situation' compared to oil which 'surprised him' on the upside.
items.angle: When a macro trader who is playing the Hormuz oil story specifically prefers Nat Gas over oil, that's a positioning signal worth noting.
items.tweetA: Dale Pinkert has been long Nat Gas for 3 straight weeks. 'Never sell a sleeping market and it was sleeping for a quarter. So this is where I'm long.' He's NOT trading oil despite the Hormuz story. Nat Gas is his lower-risk play in the energy complex. Nobody talks about it. That's usually a feature.
items.tweetB: While everyone watches WTI at $101 and the Hormuz headlines, a technically-driven macro trader has quietly been long Nat Gas for 3 weeks. The thesis: sleeping market, Q1 consolidation, breakout. 'Nobody likes it.' $UNG $BOIL

charts.desc: Nat Gas chart showing breakout from quarterly consolidation

items.title: Hormuz fertilizer disruption: 1/3 of global seaborne fertilizer blocked during spring planting
items.idea: George Noble (Noble Capital Advisors): the Iran standoff over the Strait of Hormuz has no near-term resolution, with 1,600 vessels trapped and 20% of global oil supply at risk. Critically, roughly one-third of global seaborne fertilizer trade is constrained during the critical spring planting season. Quarterly earnings reports have not yet reflected the impact. The forward oil curve is implying near-term resolution that Noble says is unjustified.
items.angle: The fertilizer disruption is a 6-12 month food inflation story that the bond market is not pricing and the stock market hasn't reflected in ag/food company estimates.
items.tweetA: Buried in the Hormuz story: 1/3 of global seaborne fertilizer is currently blocked. During spring planting. Noble argues quarterly earnings haven't reflected this yet. The oil market is already screaming. The food market hasn't started yet. Fertilizer companies, grain futures, food inflation — all underpriced.
items.tweetB: Noble on gold miners: GDX gained 94% over the past year vs Nasdaq +34%. GLD holdings are BELOW decade-ago levels despite gold trading far higher. Central banks buying aggressively, Western retail still not in. 'I'll turn bearish on gold when fiat policy reverses.' It hasn't. $GDX $GLD

items.title: GDX +94% last year vs Nasdaq +34% — GLD holdings still below decade-ago levels, miners cheap
items.idea: George Noble: GDX gained 94% over the past year vs 34% for the Nasdaq. The longer-term GDX-to-QQQ ratio still shows miners trailing badly. GLD holdings sit below levels from a decade ago when gold traded far lower. Noble argues mining-sector de-equitization through buybacks and dividend hikes mirrors the Mag 7 dynamic and will tighten float. He's long SSR Mining: pure North American producer after selling its Turkish asset for $1.3B, $3B in cash, trades at ~7x earnings, ~70% margins, 10% of float buyback authorized.
items.angle: Western retail has not chased this move. Institutional ownership of miners is thin. If retail discovers the gold miner trade after a year of 94% gains, the re-rating from here could be substantial.
items.tweetA: GDX +94% last year. Nasdaq +34%. And yet GLD holdings are BELOW where they were a decade ago when gold was far cheaper. Central banks buying. Western retail asleep. Noble's thesis: mining de-equitization through buybacks mirrors the Mag 7. Cheap earnings, thin ownership, accelerating buybacks. $GDX
items.tweetB: Noble's long: SSR Mining. Sold Turkish asset for $1.3B, pure North American now, $3B cash, 7x earnings, ~70% margins, buying back 10% of float. The gold miner playbook is simple: buy cheap cash flow, let the gold price do the work. The 94% GDX year hasn't brought in retail yet.

items.title: Gold targets $3,500 on downside; silver in 'washout phase' — gold-silver ratio just reversed sharply
items.idea: Pinkert: the gold-silver ratio plummeted and then reversed sharply this week — 'that's what's giving us the turn where silver should really underperform gold for a while.' He's in the sub-$50 camp for silver and says the weekly chart 'looks like the market beginning its washout phase.' For gold, targets around $3,500, and he'd 'sell any rallies' and 'buy weakness in the dollar.' Platinum broke down again, targeting $1,400 from current ~$1,979.
items.angle: The gold-silver ratio reversal is a technical signal that often precedes a leg lower in silver. If the washout hits, gold at $3,500 is a ~23% decline from recent highs — worth having a level in mind.
items.tweetA: Gold-silver ratio plummeted then violently reversed this week. Pinkert reads this as: silver underperforms gold from here. Silver entering 'washout phase' on the weekly. His target: sub-$50. Gold: ~$3,500 downside target. Platinum target: $1,400 from $1,979. The precious metals complex is NOT a monolith right now.
items.tweetB: Pinkert would sell gold rallies and buy dollar weakness. His gold target on the downside: ~$3,500. That would be a ~23% correction from the highs — consistent with every prior gold bull market consolidation. The gold bull is intact. The near-term correction is not done.

charts.desc: Gold-silver ratio chart showing sharp reversal

items.title: Stornetta (Bitcoin whitepaper co-cited): seeing and hearing no longer constitute believing — deepfakes are the Arup problem
items.idea: Scott Stornetta, whose work on tamper-proof data integrity was cited in the original Bitcoin whitepaper, argues deepfakes are the most pressing near-term AI risk. His analogy: as planes flew higher through storms, pilots had to abandon visual flying for instruments because the vestibular system gave wrong readings. Society now faces the same transition — seeing and hearing can no longer be trusted. The Arup case: a Hong Kong employee transferred funds based on a Zoom call where every senior officer except the victim was a deepfake. His company SureMark addresses this through positive credential assertion rather than deepfake detection. He also predicts the kilowatt-hour becomes the natural medium of exchange for machine-to-machine transactions.
items.angle: The credential-based identity solution is the important part. Deepfake detection is a cat-and-mouse game. Positive identity assertion through cryptographic credentials is architecturally unbeatable. This is an underappreciated security infrastructure investment theme.
items.tweetA: The man cited in Satoshi's original Bitcoin whitepaper has a warning: 'Seeing and hearing no longer constitute believing.' The Arup case: every person on a Zoom call except the victim was a deepfake. $25M transferred. His solution is not detection — it's positive cryptographic identity. The deepfake problem requires a blockchain-era answer.
items.tweetB: Stornetta's long-term prediction: machines transacting with machines will settle in kilowatt-hours. Not dollars, not Bitcoin — kWh. Universal applicability. Inherent utility. Gradually deflationary. Jensen Huang frames Bitcoin as a battery for surplus energy. Stornetta says energy itself becomes the money.

items.title: Medicare ACCESS model opens reimbursement path for AI care — always-on voice agents for chronic disease
items.idea: Medicare's ACCESS model rewards patient outcomes instead of clinician hours, creating a path for reimbursable AI services. Companies like Pair Team are deploying always-on voice agents to manage chronic care with lower operating costs. This is the regulatory unlock that AI healthcare companies have been waiting for — moving from 'nice to have' to 'reimbursable clinical tool.' The model shifts incentives from volume of care to quality of outcomes.
items.angle: Medicare reimbursement is the key that unlocks healthcare AI at scale in the US. Without reimbursement, AI health tools are limited to direct-pay markets. With it, the total addressable market expands to the entire insured population.
items.tweetA: Medicare quietly opened the door to AI care reimbursement. The ACCESS model pays for outcomes, not hours — which means an always-on AI voice agent managing your chronic disease is now a reimbursable service. This is the regulatory unlock that changes healthcare AI from a startup experiment to a system-wide deployment.
items.tweetB: The Pair Team model: always-on voice AI for chronic care management, paid by Medicare based on patient outcomes. At scale, this replaces expensive care coordinator labor with 24/7 AI coverage at a fraction of the cost. The regulatory gate just opened. The healthcare AI buildout just got a reimbursement pathway.

items.title: Karsan: the Iran conflict is really a contest over dollar hegemony, not nuclear proliferation
items.idea: Cem Karsan argues the underlying conflict driving the Iran situation is not nuclear proliferation but dollar hegemony — the moves on Iran, Venezuela, and Greenland all reflect a larger contest over global leverage and the 'next 50 years of geopolitical power.' Trump's visit to Beijing with Nvidia and Tesla CEOs suggests Chinese capital commitments to US manufacturers and possible tariff reduction tied to capex investment. But any near-term China deal delivers corporate profits without resolving Hormuz.
items.angle: If the Hormuz standoff is fundamentally about dollar hegemony rather than Iran's nuclear program, it has no near-term resolution path. The conflict management industry (diplomats, ceasefire negotiators) doesn't have the tools for a dollar hegemony dispute.
items.tweetA: Karsan's framework: the Iran standoff is not about nuclear proliferation. It's about dollar hegemony. The moves on Iran, Venezuela, and Greenland are part of one coherent contest over the next 50 years of geopolitical power. Any 'Iran deal' that doesn't address the underlying dollar question is just a pause.
items.tweetB: Trump went to Beijing with Nvidia and Tesla CEOs. Karsan: China commits capital to US manufacturers, gets tariff reduction. But Hormuz stays blocked because that's a dollar hegemony dispute, not a trade dispute. Corporate profits improve. The commodity supply shock continues. Both can be true.

items.title: PEPTAI: autonomous AI agents for peptide drug discovery on-chain (Bio Protocol, Base)
items.idea: Bio Protocol launched the PEPTAI token on Base, deploying a fleet of autonomous AI agents for peptide drug discovery. This is one of the first on-chain deployments of fully autonomous AI agents for scientific research. Grok simultaneously integrated Nous Research's Hermes Agent for direct in-app deployment via subscriptions — another step toward consumer-accessible autonomous agents.
items.angle: Autonomous agents for drug discovery is the DeSci + AI convergence that has been discussed theoretically for two years. A live deployed example on Base is a category-defining moment.
items.tweetA: Bio Protocol just launched PEPTAI on Base: a fleet of autonomous AI agents for peptide drug discovery. On-chain. Live. Not a whitepaper. This is the DeSci + AI convergence we've been hearing about. Autonomous agents doing actual science. The first inning of something very strange and very important.
items.tweetB: Autonomous AI agents for drug discovery, deployed on-chain via Base. The peptide discovery market is enormous — and the agent does the work that previously required a lab and a PhD. PEPTAI is either a narrative play or the opening move of decentralized biotech. Worth watching.

items.title: High-trust vs low-trust societies: Japan vs the West — 'decline is a choice'
items.idea: A reflection on high-trust societies (Japan, Taiwan, South Korea, Singapore) vs low-trust Western cities, triggered by a viral story of a MacBook left on a Tokyo park bench being protected by a stranger. The author argues the West's trust decline correlates with civilizational decline, and that homogeneity, strong law enforcement, or surveillance all produce high-trust outcomes through different mechanisms. Conclusion: 'Decline is a choice. I, for one, choose to fight the decline all around us. It's almost always just a matter of will.'
items.angle: Trust is the invisible infrastructure of economies. High-trust societies have lower transaction costs, higher capital allocation efficiency, and better public goods provision. The measurable economic gap between Japan and Western cities on trust-dependent metrics (crime, property rights, contract enforcement) compounds over decades.
items.tweetA: Someone left a MacBook on a Tokyo park bench. Found it 3 hours later, wrapped in a plastic bag and taped to the bench to keep it dry. In most Western cities: gone. Trust is not a feeling — it's infrastructure. And the West is watching its infrastructure deteriorate in real time.
items.tweetB: The economic case for high-trust societies: lower transaction costs, better contract enforcement, higher capital efficiency, stronger public goods. Japan, Taiwan, Singapore score high. Most Western cities: declining. 'Decline is a choice' is the uncomfortable thesis. The economic data backs it up.
